findings.description: range(total_chunks - 1) drops the final chunk on every assembly, so is_complete swears all N arrived and then the assembler ceremonially truncates the file — and the checksum you so proudly added will fail every single time.

client-supplied filename is os.path.join'd into UPLOAD_DIR with zero sanitization, so '../../etc/cron.d/pwned' is an arbitrary file write — congratulations on the remote-code-execution-as-a-service feature.
upload_id flows unsanitized into the chunk path too, so a slash or '..' lets an attacker scribble chunks wherever the process can reach or collide other uploads' chunks at will.
MAX_FILE_MB and file_size_ok exist purely for decoration — receive_chunk and assemble never enforce any size, so unbounded chunk bytes and attacker-declared total_chunks fill the disk (textbook DoS).
the extension allow-list trusts the filename string and never sniffs content, so a polyglot or a name like 'shell.pdf' sails right through while being whatever the attacker wants.
content = f.read() slurps the entire file — which your own docstring says can be 200MB+ — into RAM just to hash it, instead of streaming via hashlib in chunks; enjoy the OOM under concurrency.

the chunk write has no error handling, so a disk-full or permission error raises raw and leaves the session in a half-received state with orphaned chunk files — 'graceful degradation' clearly meant something else to you.
start() blindly overwrites any existing in-flight session for the same upload_id, silently orphaning previously received chunks on disk and corrupting concurrent uploads sharing an id.
dest_path is derived solely from filename with no collision handling, so two uploads named 'report.pdf' cheerfully clobber each other — last writer wins, data loss for everyone else.

checksum comparison is a plain != on hex strings rather than a constant-time compare, and nothing ever calls verify_checksum or file_size_ok in the assembly path anyway, so both 'safety' checks are opt-in suggestions.
